dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit M75 for a priority 1 unconscious call at an apartment in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Darren, an EMT with the rescue squad. How long has he been like this?
He's been unconscious for about 1 hour. We heard him groan earlier, then he fell back and hasn't woken up.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name? We're assessing the patient's responsiveness.
His eyes are open but not responsive to normal surroundings. He's opening his eyes on verbal stimulus alone.
He's moaning but doesn't seem to understand any commands.
Can you ask him to squeeze your hand to check motor response?
Airway is patent. I don't see any visible obstructions or signs of trauma.
Breathing is spontaneous but labored. He's making some wheezing and ronchi sounds.
Respiratory rate is 19-shallow with wheezing and ronchi still evident.
Saturation is 73% in room air. We'll need to give oxygen to improve that.
Pulse is 94 and regular. Skin is warm, clammy with no signs of active bleeding.
Blood pressure is 127 over 61. No immediate signs of hypotension.
Palpation confirms pulse of 94 and regular. Still a bit fast, but not alarming.
Blood sugar is 160. Elevated, but not yet hypertonic.
He started with back pain, then all of a sudden, his status changed and he passed out.
According to us, he went to lie down and was sleeping but never woke up after.
He has long-standing history of diabetes and hypertension.
He has three drug allergies: Aspirin, Motrin, and Trazodone.
Taking Metformin, Glypizide, and Clonidine as usual.
Has he had anything to drink or eat in the past 6-8 hours?
Skin is pink and warm with no obvious cyanosis.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 13: E3V4M6. Altered mental state but motor intact.
Pupils are 3 mm in size and equally reactive.
No abnormalities in HEENT, normocephalic and no tracheal deviation.
Bilateral breath sounds with ronchi and wheezing, no asymmetrical movement.
No EKG applied yet at this time-focusing first on oxygen and breathing support.
Non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM for increased saturation.
15 LPM non-rebreather applied. Let me recheck oxygen saturation now.
Saturation has improved-now at 98% with supplemental oxygen.
Blood pressure just recorded is 123 over 66. Still stable.
Pulse is now 88, still regular.
Respiratory rate now at 15. Still labored but improving.
He also had some fever in the days prior and had some strange behavior.
Skin turgor is normal-no delayed return found during flexing or pinching.
IV site was difficult but established on left side after a few attempts.
Applying 4-lead ECG monitoring now.
Let me set up the ECG leads to monitor for possible cardiac abnormalities.
Administering 100 mL Normal Saline. No major resistance at IV site.
Following protocol: Altered Mental Status (3-15); Respiratory Distress (3-11).
No arrhythmias observed now-4-lead monitoring confirms sinus rhythm per ePCR.
Still at 98%. The non-rebreather is effective and making a real impact.
The patient is still not reporting pain. Per ePCR, no pain currently.
Rechecked blood sugar-still elevated at 160 ppm.
Transporting to VCU Medical Center per his request. Condition is stable.
Using a Reeves stretcher for safe transfer. Transport is about to begin.
Full padding applied to all sides. He's now secure for transport.
He will be alright going to the hospital, right?
Once at the hospital in EDTA Room 10, he will receive further care upon registration.
He is now in the hospital bed and registered. Nurses will take over from here.
IV and O2 in place. He is stable for transport now.
IV and O2 will be maintained. Notes with allergies and meds already handed over.
Recheck EKG-sinus rhythm recorded second time per ePCR. Still no irregularities.
IV site still patent. No swelling or infiltration observed.
Saline remained patent. No further bolus given as he's stable.
No visible signs of head or neck scarring are found.
Oral cavity found to be free of obstructions or strange foreign items.
Let's keep him in a near-upright position due to low initial SatO2 to prevent positional hypoxia.
Bed stable with lock engaged. Preparing final Velcro for transport safety.
Must we do anything for him at hospital entrance?
NO. Hospital team will handle the entrance. Our job is done at this transfer.
All EKG leads and IV are functioning at hospital. No signs of pain or distress now.
Head to toe secured. Sheet hauling set. All systems go for hospital entry.
Still at Spo2 98%. No drop while we were loaded into and arrived at the hospital.
Let's just do an ETCO2 test one last time before unload. Shows end-tidal CO2 in mid 40s.
Moving him in now. Smooth but careful transfer per hospital bed prep.
All secured for hospital embarkation. Go ahead ready. No warning lights or hiccups.
Double confirmed in ePCR-4-lead ECG with sinus rhythm. Still clear.
That's all from our side. He's safe in the hands of hospital medical team now.
He is stabilized on the hospital bed. All documentation matched at handover.
Patient's vitals still stable. No change before or during transport.
Free and clear from EMS. Hospital is handling intake. Our job is over.
